questions.passage:
Every so often a scandal erupts over the discovery that some celebrated memoir or statesman's speech was written by another hand, and the public performs its ritual dismay. The name on the cover, it turns out, did not write the book. Fraud, cries the reviewer; deception, echoes the reader who feels somehow cheated. I confess I find the outrage harder to share than to explain, for it rests on a notion of authorship that is both historically recent and, on inspection, largely a fiction.

Consider how much of what we admire was never the work of a single pen. The great cathedrals bear no author's name. The ballads and folktales that shaped whole literatures were shaped in turn by countless anonymous retellings. Statesmen have always had their speechwriters, monarchs their secretaries, and the letters of the powerful were routinely drafted by clerks and merely signed above. The lone author, laboring in solitude and answerable for every word, is not the natural condition of writing but a specific and rather late invention, bound up with the printed book, the marketplace, and the laws that made a text into a piece of property with an owner.

What the ghostwriter does, then, is not to violate some ancient rule but to make visible a collaboration that has always been present and usually hidden. The politician who hires a writer to shape his memoir is doing openly what editors, researchers, and assistants do quietly in the making of nearly every book that bears a single name. The acknowledgments page is a monument to this concealed labor, a list of the people without whom the credited author could not have produced the work, thanked in small print precisely so that the fiction of sole authorship may stand undisturbed on the cover.

The defender of the byline will object that the reader is owed honesty, that to buy a book under one name written by another is to be sold a false bill of goods. But this assumes the transaction is a promise about who moved the pen, when it is really a promise about whose thought, whose experience, whose voice the book delivers. A statesman's ghostwritten memoir can be entirely faithful to his life and views, more faithful, perhaps, than he could have made it unaided, since the writer's craft serves the subject's meaning. What the reader is owed is that the book be true to the person it presents, not that the person have been physically capable of composing it.

None of this is to deny that ghostwriting can be abused. A memoir that fabricates a life, that puts convictions in a mouth that never held them, is a genuine fraud, but the fraud lies in the falsehood, not in the collaboration. We do not condemn a portrait because the sitter did not wield the brush, nor a building because the client did not lay the bricks. The name attached to a work has always signified responsibility for it, authority over it, ownership of its meaning, rather than the bare physical fact of its composition, and we deceive ourselves when we pretend otherwise.

The persistent fantasy that a book should issue whole from one solitary mind tells us less about how writing works than about how we wish it worked. We want the author to be a single responsible self so that we may admire, blame, and above all identify him, and the ghostwriter offends chiefly by reminding us that the self on the cover was always, to some degree, a collaboration we agreed not to see. The scandal is not that some books have hidden authors. It is that we ever believed the rest did not.
questions.stem: The author's central contention is that

choices.A: readers should stop buying any book that carries a single author's name
choices.B: ghostwriting is always a form of fraud that deceives the reading public
choices.C: no book has ever been genuinely written by a single individual
choices.D: outrage at ghostwriting rests on a recent and largely fictional idea of solitary authorship

explanations.A: The author never advises boycotting single-author books; the argument targets a mistaken idea of authorship, not the purchase of such books.
explanations.B: The author explicitly separates genuine fraud, a fabricated life, from collaboration as such, insisting the fraud lies in the falsehood, not in the ghostwriting, so ghostwriting is not equated with fraud.
explanations.C: The claim is that sole authorship is often a convenient fiction and collaboration usually present, not the absolute that no book was ever written by one person.
explanations.D: Correct. The author argues the outrage rests on a notion of authorship that is both historically recent and largely a fiction, and that ghostwriting merely makes visible a collaboration always present. Deflating the myth of solitary authorship is the through-line.

questions.stem: The author cites cathedrals, ballads, and clerks' letters chiefly to

choices.A: show that admired works have often had no single author, making solitary authorship the exception
choices.B: argue that anonymous work is always superior to signed work
choices.C: prove that writing did not exist before the invention of the printed book
choices.D: demonstrate that folktales and old ballads were originally composed by paid professional ghostwriters working for hire

explanations.A: Correct. The examples support the claim that the lone author is not the natural condition of writing but a late invention, since cathedrals bear no author's name and the letters of the powerful were drafted by clerks and merely signed. They establish that collective or hidden authorship is the norm.
explanations.B: The author is not ranking anonymous work above signed work; the point is that single authorship is often a fiction, not that anonymity is superior.
explanations.C: The passage ties the lone-author idea to the printed book but does not claim writing itself began with print; ballads and letters predate the concept.
explanations.D: Folktales are described as shaped by countless anonymous retellings, the opposite of composition by professional ghostwriters.

questions.stem: The passage describes the acknowledgments page as 'a monument to this concealed labor' in order to suggest that

choices.A: most authors refuse to thank anyone who helped them
choices.B: acknowledgments pages are unnecessary and should be abolished
choices.C: the hidden collaborators are credited only in small print so sole authorship can stand on the cover
choices.D: the people thanked in the notes actually did far more of the writing than the credited author did in every single case

explanations.A: The point is that authors do thank collaborators, but in small print; it is not that authors refuse to acknowledge help.
explanations.B: The author uses the acknowledgments to reveal hidden collaboration, not to argue the page should be abolished.
explanations.C: Correct. The author calls the acknowledgments a list of the people without whom the credited author could not have produced the work, thanked in small print precisely so that the fiction of sole authorship may stand undisturbed on the cover, exposing the concealment.
explanations.D: The passage says collaborators were essential, not that they always did more writing than the author; that overstates the claim.

questions.stem: The tone of the passage is best characterized as

choices.A: anxious and apologetic about defending an indefensible practice
choices.B: provocative and contrarian, challenging a reaction the author regards as misplaced
choices.C: detached and impartial, favoring neither side of the dispute
choices.D: nostalgic for an age when authors wrote entirely alone

explanations.A: The author is assertive rather than apologetic, arguing the practice is widely misunderstood rather than defending something conceded to be indefensible.
explanations.B: Correct. The author opens by confessing the public outrage is hard to share, then presses a deliberately against-the-grain case that ghostwriting merely reveals ordinary collaboration, closing with the pointed reversal that the scandal is our ever believing books had single authors. The stance is provocative and contrarian.
explanations.C: The passage clearly takes a side, deflating the byline defenders' position, so it is not impartial.
explanations.D: The author denies that solitary authorship was ever the norm, so nostalgia for an age of writing alone contradicts the argument.

questions.stem: The author's comparison to a portrait whose sitter did not wield the brush is meant to support the claim that

choices.A: a name on a work signifies responsibility and ownership of meaning, not the physical act of making it
choices.B: portraits are more honest than memoirs because their authorship is obvious
choices.C: sitters should be credited as the true authors of their portraits
choices.D: physical craftsmanship and the manual labor of composition are the only legitimate basis for attributing any work

explanations.A: Correct. The portrait and the building appear to show that we do not condemn a work because the subject or client did not do the manual labor, illustrating the author's thesis that a name signifies responsibility, authority, and ownership of meaning rather than the bare physical fact of composition.
explanations.B: The comparison is not about which medium is more honest; it uses the portrait to redefine what attribution means, not to rank portraits against memoirs.
explanations.C: The point is precisely that we do not treat the sitter as the maker; the analogy argues against equating credit with physical execution.
explanations.D: The analogy undercuts the physical-craft criterion, arguing attribution rests on responsibility for meaning, not on who performed the labor.

questions.stem: Which situation would the author most clearly classify as genuine fraud rather than acceptable collaboration?

choices.A: An editor substantially reorganizes an author's manuscript before publication
choices.B: A statesman hires a skilled writer to shape a memoir faithful to his own views
choices.C: A memoir attributes to its subject convictions and experiences he never held
choices.D: A researcher gathers the sources on which a credited author draws

explanations.A: Editorial reorganization is the ordinary hidden labor the author defends, part of nearly every book that bears a single name, not fraud.
explanations.B: A ghostwritten memoir faithful to the subject's views is the author's model of legitimate collaboration, said to be perhaps more faithful than an unaided attempt, not fraud.
explanations.C: Correct. The author allows that ghostwriting can be abused and names the abuse precisely: a memoir that fabricates a life or puts convictions in a mouth that never held them is a genuine fraud, because the fraud lies in the falsehood, not in the collaboration.
explanations.D: A researcher gathering sources is exactly the concealed collaboration the passage treats as normal and honest, not as deception.
